title: International Trade and Investment
gist: This is the NBER International Trade and Investment program landing page, which LISTS many recent working papers with short abstracts rather than presenting a single paper. Notable findings cover 2025 tariff impacts and pass-through, EV/battery industrial policy, Smoot-Hawley evidence, AI-related trade and adoption, the trade-conflict (peace-dividend) link, and immigration shocks; each insight captures one paper's finding.

themes.theme: 2025 tariff regime: scale, incidence, and pass-through

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35064) by Fajgelbaum & Khandelwal reports the US raised average tariff duties from 2.4% to 9.6% in 2025, bringing protectionism to its highest level in eighty years.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies the 2025 tariff jump and its short-run impacts — a core input for inflation and trade-flow forecasts.
insights.quote: the U.S. raised average tariff duties from 2.4% to 9.6%, bringing protectionism to its highest level in eighty years

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34901) by Ganapati & Hottman shows transaction-level quantity discounts in US trade reflect scale economies rather than market power, shaping tariff incidence and producing incomplete pass-through.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Mechanism for who actually pays tariffs — scale-economy discounts mute pass-through to US buyers.
insights.quote: these discounts reflect transaction-level scale economies rather than market power
insights.locator: Working Paper 34901 — Did Foreigners Pay America's Tariffs?

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35023) by Heeney, Knittel & Mandia analyzes how tariffs on vehicles and parts operate through global value chains in US automobiles, beyond consumer substitution and firm pricing.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Auto-tariff incidence flows through input supply chains — relevant to OEM margins and parts suppliers.
insights.quote: trade policies such as tariffs on vehicles and parts operate not only through consumer substitution and firm pricing
insights.locator: Working Paper 35023 — Tariffs, Global Value Chains, and the Incidence of Protection

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35249) by Mitchener & Pedemonte estimates that in the first year after the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, imports facing rate increases fell swiftly and dramatically relative to unaffected imports.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Historical Smoot-Hawley import collapse is a cautionary base rate for the current protectionist cycle.
insights.quote: imports facing rate increases fell swiftly and dramatically relative to imports not affected
insights.locator: Working Paper 35249 — The Price of Protection

themes.theme: Trade cooperation breakdown and optimal tariffs

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35303) by Bonadio, Levchenko & Pandalai-Nayar quantitatively evaluates other countries' optimal tariffs and the prospects for sustaining trade cooperation when the US exits the cooperative regime.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Models retaliation cascades ('falling dominoes') from US trade exit — a roadmap for global tariff escalation risk.
insights.quote: Falling Dominoes? The Impact of the US Exit from Free Trade on the Sustainability of Trade Cooperation

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35334) by Barwick et al. studies optimal EV trade and industrial policy combining tariffs with revenue-funded subsidies when governments weigh environment, competitiveness, and resilience.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Policy-design template for EV protectionism balanced against green goals — affects auto/battery supply chains.
insights.quote: Optimal Electric Vehicle Tariffs with Revenue-Funded Subsidies
insights.locator: Working Paper 35334 — From Trade War to Green Transition

insights.claim: An NBER article by Head, Mayer, Melitz & Yang examines how industrial policies (US IRA, Canadian subsidies, EU tariffs on Chinese EVs) shaped the global EV and battery supply chain as governments spend billions to attract manufacturing.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Maps how subsidies/tariffs are redrawing EV-battery manufacturing geography — direct supply-chain investment signal.
insights.quote: The US Inflation Reduction Act, Canadian production subsidies, and European Union tariffs on Chinese EVs
insights.locator: How Industrial Policies Shaped the Global Electric Vehicle Supply Chain

themes.theme: AI in trade and business adoption

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35053) by Michael E. Waugh uses an LLM classification tool on US trade data to find AI-related products account for 23 percent of US imports.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies AI-infrastructure's footprint in US import flows — a tariff-exposure and supply-chain concentration signal.
insights.quote: AI-related products account for 23 percent of U.S
insights.locator: Working Paper 35053 — Trade in AI-Related Products

insights.claim: An NBER article (Firm Data on AI, WP 34836) by Yotzov et al. assembles internationally comparable data on how businesses use AI, addressing the scarcity of cross-country evidence on adoption.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Cross-country firm-level AI-adoption data is a forward indicator of where productivity gains may land.
insights.quote: internationally comparable data on how businesses use this new tool are scarce
insights.locator: Global Evidence on Business Use of AI

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35194) by Alvarez et al. studies how AI-driven data-center buildout affects local economies, noting rapid AI diffusion is intensifying demand for compute and accelerating data-center investment.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Reinforces the AI compute-demand thesis from the trade/local-economy angle.
insights.quote: The rapid diffusion of artificial intelligence (AI) is intensifying demand for compute, accelerating investment in data centers
insights.locator: Working Paper 35194 — Data Centers and Local Economies

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35078) by Feng et al. investigates the causal 'peace dividend' of international trade on interstate military conflicts using global bilateral data from 1962 to 2014.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Causal evidence that trade lowers conflict risk bears on the geopolitical cost of decoupling.
insights.quote: the causal impact of international trade on interstate military conflicts
insights.locator: Working Paper 35078 — The Peace Dividend of International Trade

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35182) by Fort et al. builds a 45-year panel of US firms' patenting and argues growth is getting harder to find, not ideas, challenging the standard 'ideas are getting harder to find' interpretation of flat productivity versus rising R&D.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Reframes the productivity-stall debate — implications for how to read flat US output growth against R&D spend.
insights.quote: Growth is Getting Harder to Find, Not Ideas

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35167) by Gu, Lileeva & Trefler challenges the conventional view that offshoring intermediates to China stimulates innovation, examining negative consequences for innovation.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: If offshoring to China dampens (not boosts) innovation, reshoring's innovation case strengthens.
insights.quote: On the Negative Consequences of Low-Wage Offshoring for Innovation

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34927) by Lee, Peri & Yang exploits South Korea's sudden 2020 COVID-era suspension of its low-skilled guest-worker program to study what happens when entry-level immigrant labor supply is abruptly reduced.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: A natural experiment on sudden immigration stops — informs labor-cost and inflation effects of restrictive immigration policy.
insights.quote: exploiting South Koreas sudden suspension of its low-skilled guest worker program following the 2020 COVID-19 border
insights.locator: Working Paper 34927 — The Effects of a Sudden Stop in Low-Skilled Immigration

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35315) by Comin, Danieli & Mestieri develops an income-channel framework for labor-market polarization, where income-elastic sectors are intensive in high- and low-skilled occupations.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: An income-driven mechanism for job polarization complements skill-biased-tech explanations relevant to wage inequality.
insights.quote: The Income Channel to Labor-Market Polarization
